channel.title: Governance Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Governance Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Founding and Governing Documents: A Survey of Constitutional Instruments
item.description:
¶What This Is

Organizations come into being through documents. A founding document constitutes a community—it declares who the community is, what authority governs it, what rules bind its members, and what processes must be followed for the community to act with legitimacy. A governing document specifies the operational rules that flow from that constitution.

The distinction matters because it maps onto different legal and institutional roles. The same organization may require both a charter (the act of constitution granted by an external authority) and bylaws (the internal operating rules). Or it may require articles of incorporation (the legal charter created by the incorporators themselves) plus bylaws. Or it may be governed by a treaty (an agreement between sovereign entities) with no subordinate documents.

item.title: The Temporality of Normative Systems
item.description: Every normative system — the structure of norms, duties, mandates, and governance that constrains human action — depends on temporal structure. Without explicit temporal boundaries, validity periods, discharge conditions, and recurrence patterns, governance systems collapse in practice. They may work on paper, with rules written down and authority structures documented, but they fail where time matters: in succession, in accountability after office, in the legitimacy of normative change, in the coordination of recurring decisions.

item.title: Black Radical Governance
item.description:
¶What This Is

The Black radical tradition is not merely a historical phenomenon—it is a coherent theory of governance that begins from a premise foreign to the dominant Western political tradition: that all existing governance systems carry the inheritance of racial domination, and that formal equality operating within inherited structures reproduces that domination at deeper levels. This survey examines how five foundational thinkers—Cedric Robinson, Ruth Wilson Gilmore, Saidiya Hartman, Fred Moten and Stefano Harney, and W.E.B. Du Bois—construct a theory of governance that locates the problem not merely in bad rulers or unjust laws, but in the historical embedding of racial ordering within the very structures through which governance operates.

item.title: Cybernetics and Governance: Feedback, Variety, and Self-Regulation
item.description:
¶The Absent Tradition

The governance domain is currently formalized almost entirely through deontic logic, Hohfeldian positions, and constitutional normativity — frameworks that define governance by asking: What rules are valid? Who has authority to make decisions? What duties and rights are normatively prescribed? These are essential questions. But governance in living institutions must also answer different ones: How does a system sense when it is failing? How does it correct course? What must a governance system contain within itself to regulate a world more complex than any single authority can comprehend?

item.title: Feminist Organizational Theory: A Survey of How Governance Reproduces Gender Inequality
item.description: Mainstream organizational theory treats structures—hierarchies, roles, accountability chains, decision procedures—as if they operate on genderless agents. Feminist organizational theory rejects this fiction. The five strands covered here argue that governance structures are gendered at their foundations: they encode assumptions about what counts as work, who is capable of authority, what labor is visible and compensated, and who bears the costs of the organization’s functioning. Governance does not simply reflect gender inequality that exists elsewhere; governance actively produces it through formal procedures that appear neutral.

item.title: Indigenous Governance Traditions Beyond State Sovereignty
item.description:
¶Overview

The governance traditions of indigenous peoples across the world offer sophisticated normative systems grounded in kinship, reciprocal obligation, ecological embeddedness, and consensus decision-making. Rather than innovations in modern political theory, these are documented, internally coherent systems with centuries or millennia of institutional practice. They challenge the assumption that state-centric, individual-rights-based governance frameworks represent the only mature model of social order.

This survey examines five major governance traditions: the Haudenosaunee Confederacy of the Eastern Woodlands; Anishinaabe clan governance; Ubuntu philosophy from Southern Africa; the Andean ayllu system; and Aboriginal Australian kinship governance. Each represents a distinct approach to organizing authority, making decisions, and maintaining accountability—and collectively, they expose blind spots in frameworks that treat governance primarily as the exercise of legal authority by sovereign entities over individual rights-holders.

item.title: Institutional logics in organizations
item.description:
¶Introduction

The governance domain treats normative systems and institutions as relatively stable structures: a set of norms that constrain and enable behavior within a bounded domain. But organizations and fields rarely operate under a single normative regime. A hospital must simultaneously satisfy patient care logics, medical research logics, financial/efficiency logics, and state regulatory logics. A public university navigates academic prestige logics, market competition logics, public service logics, and state funding logics. A professional services firm operates within market logics, professional logics, and corporate logics. What happens when these systems make conflicting demands?

item.title: Social Role Theory: A Survey of Competing Accounts
item.description:
This survey traces the major traditions that define what a role is, from early sociological formulations through computational formalizations. Each tradition contributes something the others lack: functional theory explains why roles exist, interactionist theory explains how they are performed, structural theory explains how they relate to each other, and normative multi-agent theory gives the formal machinery to reason about them.

¶The sociological foundations

¶Linton and Parsons: status, role, and normative expectation

Ralph Linton (The Study of Man, 1936) introduced the foundational status/role distinction: status is a collection of rights and duties; a role is the dynamic aspect of status — the enactment of those rights and duties. Talcott Parsons (The Social System, 1951) adopted and extended this framework, making role-expectations the central unit of social analysis. In Parsons’ formulation, a role is defined by a set of normatively governed orientations that ego has toward alter, embedded in reciprocal normative expectations across a social system. Roles are not descriptions of typical behavior but prescriptions — standards whose authority is independent of compliance. Every role comes with internal standards of excellent performance; the same acts can be evaluated as excellent, adequate, or deficient by reference to the role’s standards.
